1: wayyāqom ʾaḥărê ʾăḇîmeleḵ ləhôšîaʿ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl tôlāʿ ben-pûʾāh ben-dôḏô ʾîš yiśśāśkār — 'After Abimelech, Tola the son of Puah, son of Dodo, a man of Issachar, rose to save Israel.' ləhôšîaʿ (H3467, to save/deliver) — the judge's essential function stated succinctly; qûm (H6965, to arise/rise up) is the standard verb for YHWH raising up a deliverer. Tola's genealogy (son of Puah, son of Dodo) goes three generations — unusual depth for a minor judge, suggesting scribal memory of an actual historical figure. His name means 'worm' or 'crimson/scarlet' (tôlāʿ H8438 — the same word for the crimson dye from the cochineal insect, used in the tabernacle textiles, Exodus 25:4).
3: wayyāqom ʾaḥărāyw yāʾîr haggileʿādî wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl ʿeśrîm ûšəṭayim šānāh — 'After him arose Jair the Gileadite, who judged Israel twenty-two years.' šāpaṭ (H8199, to judge) — the minor judges are described by this verb without accompanying military narrative. gilʿādî (Gileadite — from Gilead, H1568, the Transjordan tribal territory) — Jair's origin in the Transjordan is consistent with the geographic breadth of Israel's settlement. His thirty sons riding on thirty donkeys (v4) — ʿayārîm H5895, young donkeys — is a status marker: donkey-riding was aristocratic in pre-horse Israel (cf. Judges 5:10 — those who ride on white donkeys). The thirty cities named 'Havvoth-jair' (H2334) are his legacy, preserving his name in the landscape.
6: wayyōsîpû bənê yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bəʿênê YHWH wayyaʿabdû ʾet-habbəʿālîm wəʾet-hāʿaštārôt wəʾet ʾĕlōhê ʾărām wəʾet ʾĕlōhê ṣîḏôn wəʾet ʾĕlōhê môʾāḇ wəʾet ʾĕlōhê bənê ʿammôn wəʾet ʾĕlōhê pəlištîm — the longest and most explicit list of foreign deities in Judges. Seven specific national pantheons are named: bəʿālîm (Baals — plural form of Baal H1168, the storm/fertility deity), ʿaštārôt (Ashtaroth — H6252, plural of Ashtoreth/Astarte, the goddess of love and war), ʾĕlōhê ʾărām (gods of Aram — Syrian pantheon including Hadad), ʾĕlōhê ṣîḏôn (gods of Sidon — Phoenician deities including Baal and Asherah), ʾĕlōhê môʾāḇ (gods of Moab — primarily Chemosh H3645), ʾĕlōhê ʿammôn (gods of Ammon — primarily Molech/Milcom H4445), ʾĕlōhê pəlištîm (gods of the Philistines — primarily Dagon H1712). The exhaustive list emphasizes the comprehensiveness of Israel's apostasy: every surrounding people's deity has been embraced.
7: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bəyiśrāʾēl wayyimkərēm bəyaḏ-pəlištîm ûbəyaḏ bənê ʿammôn — 'The anger of YHWH burned against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines and into the hands of the sons of Ammon.' ḥārāh ʾap (H2734 H639, the anger/nose burned — idiom for divine wrath) — the burning-nose idiom for anger is physiological: flared nostrils, flushed face. The dual oppression — Philistines (west) AND Ammon (east/Transjordan) — means Israel is attacked from two directions simultaneously, the most severe judgment yet. mākar (H4376, to sell) — again the commercial metaphor for YHWH's judicial action: Israel's apostasy is a breach of covenant that results in covenant curses (Deuteronomy 28:36 — 'YHWH will bring you and your king to a nation that neither you nor your fathers have known').
10: wayyiṣʿaqû bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH lēʾmōr ḥāṭāʾnû lāk kî-ʿāzaḇnû ʾet-ʾĕlōhênû wanaʿăḇōd ʾet-habbəʿālîm — 'The sons of Israel cried out to YHWH, saying: We have sinned against you, for we have forsaken our God and served the Baals.' This is the most explicit corporate confession in Judges: ḥāṭāʾnû lāk (we have sinned to/against you — the covenant-violation formula), ʿāzaḇ (H5800, to forsake/abandon — the covenant desertion term opposite of dāḇaq to cling/cleave to, Deuteronomy 10:20), ʿāḇad (H5647, to serve — the word for both slave-labor and worship; to worship Baal is to be enslaved to Baal). The confession identifies the sin precisely: not merely wrongdoing but covenant infidelity — forsaking a person (YHWH) for a rival.
14: ləkû wəziʿaqû ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer bəḥartem bāhem hēmmāh yôšîʿû lākem bəʿēt ṣāratkêm — 'Go and cry out to the gods whom you have chosen. Let them save you in your time of distress.' YHWH's sarcasm — 'go cry to the gods you chose' — is covenant-lawsuit rhetoric (rîḇ H7379 pattern). The verb bāḥar (H977, to choose) echoes Deuteronomy's repeated assertion that Israel chose YHWH (Deuteronomy 26:17-18) and YHWH chose Israel — here the choice is inverted: Israel chose foreign gods and must now experience the logical consequence of that choice. ṣārāh (H6869, distress/trouble/straits) — the gods of Ammon/Philistia cannot deliver from ṣārāh; only YHWH, the God of the Exodus, can.
16: wayyāsîrû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēkār miqqirḇām wayyaʿabdû ʾet-YHWH wattiqqṣar napšô bəʿămal yiśrāʾēl — 'They put away the foreign gods from among them and served YHWH, and he became impatient over the misery of Israel.' ʾĕlōhê hannēkār (H5236, foreign gods — gods of the strange/alien — these are the gods of the other nations, not just any unknown deity; the term nēkār implies both foreignness and strangeness). wattiqqṣar napšô — 'his soul/being was shortened/impatient' — qāṣar (H7114, to be short/cut short; with nepeš it means to feel distress, to be grieved, to be unable to endure — cf. Numbers 21:4 where Israel's soul 'became short' on the way). YHWH is not a detached judge but a personal being capable of emotional response to human suffering. This verse is one of the most striking expressions of divine pathos in the OT: Israel's ʿāmal (H5999, toil/misery/trouble — the word for the crushing labor of oppression) registers in YHWH's own inner life.
2: wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl ʿeśrîm ûšəlōš šānāh wayyāmot wayyiqqāḇēr bəšāmîr — 'He judged Israel twenty-three years. Then he died and was buried at Shamir.' The minor judge notice for Tola follows the minimalist formula: years of service, death, burial. Šāmîr — the burial site, in the hill country of Ephraim (v1), likely a different Shamir from the Judean site (Joshua 15:48). The twenty-three years of stable judging (immediately after Abimelech's three-year catastrophe) represent covenant normalcy: the minor judges hold the land in peace, their very uneventfulness testifying to successful governance.
4: wayhî-lô šəlōšîm bānîm rōkəḇîm ʿal-šəlōšîm ʿayārîm ûšəlōšîm ʿărāyôt lāhem bəʾereṣ haggileʿāḏ wattiqqārēʾ lāhem Ḥawwôt-yāʾîr ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh — 'He had thirty sons riding on thirty donkeys, and they had thirty cities in Gilead, which are called Havvoth-jair to this day.' rōkəḇîm ʿal-ʿayārîm (riding on young donkeys) — the donkey is the royal/judicial mount (cf. Judges 5:10; Zechariah 9:9). Thirty judge-sons administering thirty cities represents either efficient decentralized justice or dynastic patronage nepotism — the text is neutral, but the pattern of judge-sons taking administrative roles signals the drift toward hereditary rule that will produce Abimelech (Judges 9:1). Ḥawwôt-yāʾîr (villages of Jair) — the place-name preserves Jair's administrative legacy (cf. Numbers 32:41).
5: wayyāmot yāʾîr wayyiqqāḇēr bəqāmôn — 'Jair died and was buried at Kamon.' The minor judge's death-and-burial notice is the most compressed in Judges: seven Hebrew words. Kamon is unidentified; the narrator records only that Jair's bones lie in an unknown Gileadite town. The brevity itself is the commentary: these judges served, died, and were buried — no monuments, no successors, no divine speeches. Their fruitfulness was the peace of their years, not the drama of their exploits.
8: wayyirʿăṣû wayyirṣəṣû ʾet-bənê yiśrāʾēl bašānāh hahîʾ šəmoneh ʿeśrēh šānāh ʾet kol-bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer bəʿēḇer hayyardēn bəʾereṣ hāʾĕmorî ʾăšer baggileʿāḏ — 'That year they shattered and crushed (wayyirʿăṣû wayyirṣəṣû) the sons of Israel — eighteen years — all the sons of Israel who were across the Jordan in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gilead.' The doubled verb rāʿaṣ…rāṣaṣ (H7492/H7533, shattered and crushed) uses intensive repetition for severe oppression — the same pair used for God's judgment on Israel's enemies in Psalms 68:21. Eighteen years is the longest oppression period in Judges (cf. Mesopotamian oppression, Judges 3:8 — eight years; Moab — 18 years; Hazor — 20 years; Midian — 7 years). The Gileadite focus prepares for Jephthah's story (Judges 11).
9: wayyaʿaḇrû bənê-ʿammôn ʾet-hayyardēn lěhilāḥem gam-bîhûḏāh ûḇinyāmîn ûḇəḇêt ʾeprayim wayyēṣar ləyiśrāʾēl meʾōd — 'The sons of Ammon also crossed the Jordan to fight against Judah and Benjamin and the house of Ephraim, so that Israel was greatly distressed.' The Ammonite crossing of the Jordan inverts the conquest narrative: Israel crossed the Jordan to take the land (Josh 3-4); now the enemy crosses the Jordan to take it back. The tribal list (Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim) shows the oppression spreading westward from Gilead — the entire covenant territory is threatened. wayyēṣar ləyiśrāʾēl meʾōd (Israel was greatly distressed) — the phrase triggers the next element of the cycle: Israel crying out to YHWH (v10).
11: wayyōʾmer YHWH ʾel-bənê yiśrāʾēl halōʾ mimmitsrayim ûmin-hāʾĕmorî ûmibnê ʿammôn ûmin-pəlištîm — 'YHWH said to the sons of Israel: Was it not from the Egyptians and from the Amorites and from the sons of Ammon and from the Philistines...' YHWH's direct speech to Israel is extremely rare in Judges (cf. Judges 2:1-3; Judges 6:8-10); the divine address here is a covenant lawsuit (rîḇ) listing the deliverances that create obligation. The exodus from Egypt heads the list — the foundational deliverance that grounds all subsequent claims. The Ammonites are listed among those YHWH previously defeated, making their current success all the more pointed: the oppressor YHWH once defeated is now defeating Israel because Israel forgot YHWH.
12: wəṣîḏônîm wăʾAmālēq ûMāʿôn lāḥăṣû ʾetkem wayttsʿaqû ʾēlay wāʾôšîʿāh ʾetkem miyyāḏām — 'When the Sidonians and the Amalekites and the Maonites oppressed you, you cried out to me, and I saved you from their hand.' The list of deliverances (vv11-12) functions as YHWH's covenant resume: repeated rescue from multiple enemies constitutes a pattern of grace that Israel has repeatedly spurned. The Maonites (Meunim, Arabian tribe) and Sidonians add geographic breadth: YHWH's deliverances span Transjordan, Canaan, the Philistine coast, and Arabia. The exhaustive list makes the refusal of v13 proportional — the pattern of forgiveness has been worn out by the pattern of apostasy.
13: wəʾattem ʿăzaḇtem ʾôtî wataʿaḇdû ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm lākēn lōʾ-ʾôsîp ləhôšîaʿ ʾetkem — 'Yet you have forsaken me and served other gods; therefore I will save you no more.' ʿāzaḇ (H5800, to forsake, abandon) — the covenant-disloyalty verb; Israel forsook YHWH as a man forsakes a wife (Jeremiah 1:16, Judges 2:13). The consequence is stated with startling directness: lōʾ-ʾôsîp ləhôšîaʿ ʾetkem (I will not continue to save you) — the suspension of the saving relationship. This is the one place in Judges where YHWH explicitly refuses to act; the refusal is temporary (v16b: 'he could bear Israel's misery no longer') but it stands as the theological highpoint of the chapter: grace is not automatic; covenant abandonment has consequences.
15: wayyōʾmərû bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH ḥāṭāʾnû ʿăśēh-ʾattāh lānû kəkol-haṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkā ʾak haṣṣîlēnû nāʾ hayyôm hazzeh — 'The sons of Israel said to YHWH: We have sinned. Do to us whatever seems good in your eyes; only please deliver us this day.' The confession ḥāṭāʾnû (we have sinned) is the seventh occurrence of Israel's sin-cycle in Judges, but this time it is accompanied by genuine submission: ʿăśēh ʾattāh lānû kəkol-haṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkā (do to us whatever seems good in your eyes) — covenantal surrender of the plea, accepting YHWH's judgment as just. The grammar shifts from demand (previous cycles: 'save us') to submission. This submission, combined with the removal of foreign gods (v16a), triggers YHWH's relenting.
17: wayyiṣṣāʿăqû bənê ʿammôn wayyaḥănû baggileʿāḏ wayyēʾāsəpû bənê yiśrāʾēl wayyaḥănû hammitsəpāh — 'Then the sons of Ammon were called to arms and camped in Gilead, and the sons of Israel gathered and camped at Mizpah.' The military dispositions create the geographic tension: Ammon in Gilead, Israel at Mizpah. Hammitsəpāh (the Watchtower/Lookout) in Gilead is the same site where Jacob and Laban made their covenant (Genesis 31:49: 'YHWH watch between you and me'). Israel's camp at this covenant-witness site as they prepare to face Ammon is the narrator's spatial theology: they are gathering at the place where covenant fidelity is watched over, having just recommitted to YHWH.
18: wayyōʾmərû hāʿām śārê gilʿāḏ ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû mî hāʾîš ʾăšer yaḥēl lāhilāḥem bibnê ʿammôn yihyeh lərōʾš lekol yōšəḇê gilʿāḏ — 'The people and leaders of Gilead said to one another: Who is the man who will begin the fight against the sons of Ammon? He will be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead.' The leadership vacuum created by the apostasy (no judge raised yet) produces a conditional kingship offer: military success earns political leadership. The phrase rōʾš lekol yōšəḇê gilʿāḏ (head over all the inhabitants of Gilead) anticipates Jephthah's negotiation in Judges 11:6-11, where he demands this title before agreeing to fight. The community is offering what Jephthah will secure through hard bargaining — his marginalization (Judges 11:2-3) gives him the leverage to convert crisis into covenant appointment.
